agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle class constraints, and configurable stop duration. This feature spans multiple bounded contexts: route planning, fleet/driver management, delivery lifecycle, and customer communication.",
    "bounded_context": "Multiple contexts identified: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, stop ordering, and ETAs; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, vehicle classes, licensed capabilities, zone assignments, and shift lifecycle; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-transit, delivered, failed), zone classification, and package attributes (size, weight, vehicle class requirement); (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery windows, and customer-facing messaging. The 'Smart Route Optimization' feature is an orchestration across all four contexts, with Route Planning as the primary context.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver, beginning when they clock in/start and ending when they clock out/complete. A shift is the lifecycle boundary within which route optimization operates." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic partition of the service area. Deliveries and drivers are assigned to zones. A driver operates within one zone per shift." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated by the system to minimize total travel time/distance for a driver within their zone during their shift." },
      { "term": "Recalculation Trigger", "definition": "An event that invalidates the current optimized route and requires the system to recompute the stop sequence. Three defined triggers: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver deviation." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been accepted into the system but not yet assigned to a driver's active route or not yet attempted." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that was unsuccessful (recipient unavailable, access denied, etc.), requiring removal from the current route and potential reassignment." },
      { "term": "Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of vehicle type (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight) that determines what kinds of deliveries can be physically and legally carried. Both drivers and deliveries have a vehicle class attribute." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "The vehicle class(es) a driver is qualified and authorized to operate. A driver may hold licenses for multiple classes." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "The set of the next three upcoming delivery stops on a driver's active route. Entering this window triggers customer notification." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes system-wide but is overridable per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual GPS position or stop sequence diverges from the system-suggested route beyond a defined threshold, triggering recalculation." },
      { "term": "Driver Zone Assignment", "definition": "The association between a driver and the zone they serve during a given shift." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A driver must never be assigned a delivery that requires a vehicle class exceeding their licensed vehicle class(es).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a physical and legal constraint. A motorcycle physically cannot carry freight, and a driver without the proper license is legally prohibited from operating certain vehicles. Violating this corrupts the system's safety guarantees and creates real-world legal liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D on driver X's route: D.requiredVehicleClass is contained in X.licensedVehicleClasses."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver must only be assigned deliveries within their assigned zone during their current shift.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone boundaries define operational and geographic constraints. Cross-zone assignment would break route optimization assumptions, SLA guarantees, and potentially insurance/regulatory boundaries.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D on driver X's route during shift S: D.zone == X.assignedZone(S)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When a driver starts their shift, the system must automatically generate an optimized route from all pending deliveries in the driver's assigned zone that match their vehicle class.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business workflow policy. The company could choose to allow manual route building or delayed assignment. It is a strong process rule but not a state integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Within T seconds of shift start, the driver has an active route containing only pending deliveries from their zone that are compatible with their vehicle class."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route must be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an optimization policy. The business could choose to queue new deliveries for the next route cycle instead. It does not corrupt state if skipped — it merely reduces efficiency.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a new delivery is added to zone Z and a driver in zone Z has an active route, a recalculation event is triggered and the route is updated to include the new delivery if vehicle-class compatible."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route must be recalculated when a delivery on the route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Optimization policy. A failed delivery must be removed from the active route (that part is invariant — you cannot deliver to a failed stop), but recalculating the remaining sequence is a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "When delivery D on an active route is marked failed, D is removed from the route and the remaining stops are re-optimized."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The route must be recalculated when a driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Optimization policy. The business could alternatively flag the deviation without recalculating. This is a UX/efficiency decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "When driver deviation is detected (GPS divergence or out-of-order stop completion), a recalculation event is triggered using the driver's current position as the new starting point."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a customer communication policy. The window size (3 stops) is a business preference that could change. Not notifying does not corrupt data — it degrades customer experience.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a delivery moves into position 1, 2, or 3 on the active route and the customer has not already been notified for this delivery attempt, a notification is sent to the customer."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes per delivery stop, overridable per zone by the operations team.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a configurable operational parameter. The 4-minute default and per-zone override are business policy decisions that a VP could change at any time.",
      "testable_assertion": "ETA calculations use the zone-specific stop duration if configured; otherwise, fall back to 4 minutes. The operations team can set/update the override via an admin interface."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "A failed delivery must be removed from the driver's active route immediately upon being marked failed.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Keeping a failed delivery on an active route creates a corrupt route state — the driver would be directed to a stop that is already known to be undeliverable. This is data integrity.",
      "testable_assertion": "After a delivery is marked failed, it does not appear in any active route's stop sequence."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are 10 pending deliveries in their zone, all compatible with their vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift start event is processed",
      "then": "An optimized route is generated containing all 10 deliveries, ordered to minimize travel time/distance, and the route is visible to the driver"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops and a new delivery is added to their zone",
      "when": "The new delivery event is received",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery (if vehicle-class compatible) and the driver sees the updated route"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and marks stop #3 of 7 as failed",
      "when": "The failed delivery event is processed",
      "then": "Stop #3 is removed from the route, the remaining 6 stops are re-optimized from the driver's current position, and the driver sees the updated route"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver skips stop #2 and drives directly to stop #4",
      "when": "The system detects the deviation",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current position, incorporating all undelivered stops including the skipped stop #2"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver is 4 stops away from customer C's delivery",
      "when": "The driver completes a delivery and customer C moves into the 'next 3 stops' window (position 3)",
      "then": "Customer C receives a notification with an estimated delivery time"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A motorcycle-licensed driver is in zone A and a new freight delivery is added to zone A",
      "when": "The route recalculation runs",
      "then": "The freight delivery is NOT added to the motorcycle driver's route, and no error is thrown — the delivery remains in the pending pool for a compatible driver"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "The operations team has set the stop duration for zone B to 6 minutes",
      "when": "A route is calculated for a driver in zone B with 5 stops",
      "then": "The ETA calculation uses 6 minutes per stop (30 minutes of stop time) plus travel time, not the default 4 minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are pending deliveries in their zone but none match their vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift start event is processed",
      "then": "The driver receives an empty route (or a 'no deliveries available' state) rather than being assigned incompatible deliveries"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A customer has already been notified they are in the 'next 3 stops' window",
      "when": "A route recalculation occurs and their delivery remains within the next 3 stops",
      "then": "The customer is NOT sent a duplicate notification, but their ETA is updated if it changed"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "Two drivers are active in the same zone and a new delivery is added",
      "when": "The system determines route assignments",
      "then": "The delivery is assigned to exactly one driver (not duplicated), based on optimization criteria (proximity, load balance, vehicle class compatibility)"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A new delivery is added to a zone with no active drivers", "expected_behavior": "The delivery remains in the pending pool. No route is generated. When a driver next starts a shift in that zone, the delivery is included in their initial route." },
    { "scenario": "All remaining deliveries on a route are marked as failed", "expected_behavior": "The driver's route becomes empty. The system should notify the driver and/or dispatch that the route is complete (or all-failed) and allow shift-end or reassignment." },
    { "scenario": "A driver deviates but returns to the original route before recalculation completes", "expected_behavior": "The recalculation should still complete using the most recent position. The system should not enter a recalculation loop if the driver is close to the original path." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple recalculation triggers fire simultaneously (new delivery added AND a delivery fails)", "expected_behavior": "The system should coalesce triggers and perform a single recalculation incorporating all changes, not two sequential recalculations that could conflict." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery changes zone after being assigned to a driver's route (e.g., address correction)", "expected_behavior": "The delivery should be removed from the current route and reassigned to the correct zone's pending pool, triggering recalculation on both the original and destination zones." },
    { "scenario": "A driver has a multi-class license (motorcycle AND van) and both delivery types are pending", "expected_behavior": "The route should include all deliveries compatible with the vehicle the driver is actually operating during this shift — not all vehicles they are licensed for. The system needs to know the active vehicle, not just the license." },
    { "scenario": "The 'next 3 stops' window shifts due to recalculation — a customer was in the window but is now stop #5", "expected_behavior": "The customer who exited the window should receive an updated ETA notification explaining the delay. A new customer who entered the window should receive the standard window notification." },
    { "scenario": "A route recalculation produces a route where the driver must pass through a different zone to reach the next stop", "expected_behavior": "The route may physically traverse other zones (roads don't follow zone boundaries), but the deliveries on the route must all belong to the driver's assigned zone." },
    { "scenario": "Stop duration override is set to 0 minutes for a zone", "expected_behavior": "The system should reject 0 or negative stop durations. A minimum floor should be enforced (e.g., 1 minute) to prevent nonsensical ETAs." },
    { "scenario": "A driver starts a shift but the optimization service is unavailable", "expected_behavior": "The system should fall back to a basic ordering (e.g., nearest-first) or hold deliveries as unassigned and alert dispatch, rather than assigning no route silently." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries-per-shift throughput, and reduces labor costs through efficient time utilization. The 4-minute stop duration parameter directly affects how many stops can be scheduled per shift — misconfiguration has direct revenue impact. Vehicle class enforcement prevents costly regulatory fines and insurance claim denials.",
    "operational": "Dynamic recalculation increases dispatcher workload complexity but reduces manual intervention. Concurrent recalculation triggers must be coalesced to prevent system thrashing. The per-zone stop duration override gives operations teams granular control (urban dense areas vs rural spread). Multi-driver zone conflicts require clear assignment arbitration to avoid double-dispatching.",
    "user": "Customers receive proactive delivery notifications improving satisfaction and reducing 'where is my package' support calls. Drivers receive clear, optimized routes reducing cognitive load and decision fatigue. However, frequent recalculations mid-route could frustrate drivers — the UX must handle route changes gracefully.",
    "risk": "Vehicle class violation is the highest-risk failure mode (legal liability, physical impossibility). Race conditions in multi-trigger recalculation could produce inconsistent routes. Over-notification of customers during frequent recalculations could erode trust. GPS-based deviation detection has inherent inaccuracy (tunnels, urban canyons) that could trigger false recalculations.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Primary users — receive automated routes, must trust the optimization. Frequent recalculations and unclear deviation thresholds could reduce trust and adoption." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Receive 'next 3 stops' notifications. Better visibility improves satisfaction but duplicate/contradictory notifications erode trust." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gains per-zone configuration control (stop duration). Must monitor and tune zone parameters. Needs visibility into recalculation frequency and driver compliance." },
      { "group": "Dispatch / Fleet Managers", "impact": "Reduced manual route planning burden but need escalation paths when automation fails (service down, no compatible drivers)." },
      { "group": "Finance / Compliance", "impact": "Vehicle class enforcement is a regulatory compliance safeguard. Route efficiency directly impacts unit economics." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 62,
    "reasoning": "The spec is reasonably clear on the happy path but has several meaningful gaps. The vehicle class constraint is well-defined. The recalculation triggers are enumerated but lack important operational detail (debouncing, concurrency). The 'next 3 stops' notification is clear but lacks deduplication and exit-window behavior. The biggest gap is the distinction between a driver's licensed vehicle classes and the vehicle they are actually operating during a shift — the spec conflates these.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant — unambiguous",
      "Three recalculation triggers are clearly enumerated",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism is well-specified",
      "Customer notification window trigger is clear"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What vehicle does a multi-licensed driver actually operate during a shift? License != active vehicle.",
      "Deviation detection threshold — what constitutes 'deviation' is undefined (distance? time? skipping a stop?)",
      "Multi-driver same-zone delivery assignment arbitration — which driver gets a new delivery?",
      "Notification deduplication and exit-window behavior when recalculations shift the window",
      "Recalculation concurrency — how to handle multiple simultaneous triggers"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A driver operates one vehicle per shift and the system knows which vehicle is active",
      "Zone assignments are stable for the duration of a shift (a driver does not change zones mid-shift)",
      "The optimization algorithm's criteria (minimize distance, minimize time, balance load) are not yet specified and will be defined separately",
      "Deviation detection is GPS-based with a configurable threshold",
      "Customer notification is a one-way push (SMS/email/app) — no customer response is expected"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec says 'drivers cannot be assigned deliveries outside their licensed vehicle class' but does not distinguish between what a driver is licensed to operate and what vehicle they are actually driving during this shift. A driver licensed for both motorcycle and van can only carry van-sized deliveries if they are driving the van today. The constraint should be against the ACTIVE VEHICLE, not the license.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Compliance"],
      "options": [
        "Constrain by active vehicle for the shift (correct physical constraint)",
        "Constrain by license only (allows assignment of physically impossible deliveries if driver chose a smaller vehicle)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Constrain by active vehicle for the shift. The license is a prerequisite for operating the vehicle, but the physical capability is determined by the actual vehicle. The system should capture 'active vehicle' at shift start."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "What constitutes a 'driver deviation' from the route? The spec does not define the threshold. Is it GPS distance from the planned path? Skipping a stop in sequence? Taking an alternate road to the same next stop? Without a clear definition, the recalculation trigger could fire too aggressively (driver takes a parallel street) or too conservatively (driver goes home for lunch).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "Define deviation as completing stops out of the suggested order",
        "Define deviation as GPS position exceeding X meters from the planned path for Y minutes",
        "Both: sequence deviation AND geographic deviation as separate triggers"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Use sequence deviation (completing stops out of order or skipping a stop) as the primary trigger, with geographic deviation as a secondary trigger with a generous threshold. Sequence deviation is deterministic and unambiguous; GPS deviation requires tuning and is prone to false positives."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "When multiple drivers are active in the same zone and a new delivery arrives, the spec does not define how the system decides which driver receives the delivery. This is a critical arbitration rule — without it, the delivery could be assigned to both drivers, or to the sub-optimal driver.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "Assign to the driver with the fewest remaining stops (load balancing)",
        "Assign to the geographically nearest driver (proximity)",
        "Assign to the driver whose route insertion point minimizes total route cost (global optimization)",
        "Let dispatch manually assign (defeats automation purpose)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Use route insertion cost minimization as the primary criterion, with load balancing as a tiebreaker. This produces the best customer ETAs while preventing driver overload."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
